findings.description: HTML adds an <hr> element between the Background section and Question 1 that is not present in the PDF. This is a formatting addition, not content fabrication, but worth noting.
findings.evidence: Multiple <hr> tags appear in the HTML as visual separators between Q&A sections and at page boundaries. These are not content in the PDF but are structural formatting choices.

findings.description: Links present in the PDF are referenced in the HTML but without actual href URLs. The PDF contains hyperlinks to 'Division's website', 'training video', '(available here)', 'US Environmental Protection Agency', 'Levee Terms & Definitions (army.mil)', and 'Division's website' again. The HTML mentions these as plain text or with generic references rather than preserving the actual URLs.
findings.evidence: PDF has clickable links like 'Division's website', 'training video', '(available here)', 'US Environmental Protection Agency', 'Levee Terms & Definitions (army.mil)'. The HTML removes the hyperlink functionality for most of these, rendering them as plain text references.

findings.description: The document uses h2 for 'Background' but does not use heading tags for any of the 20 Q&A items, which could be considered section-level content. However, this is an acceptable structural decision since the Q&A items are styled with bold text inline.
findings.evidence: Only h1 (title) and h2 (Background) headings are used. Questions are marked up as bold paragraphs, which is a reasonable choice.

findings.description: The HTML contains a nested duplicate <main> element, which causes the entire content to be structurally duplicated in the DOM, though it renders as a single copy visually.
findings.evidence: The HTML has '<main>' inside another '<main>' element: the outer <main> wraps the inner <main> which contains all the content.

findings.description: The typo 'ANSWSER' from the original PDF (Question 3's answer) is faithfully preserved in the HTML, which is correct behavior.
